section.title: The Lord of All Creation
section.sectionRationale: One of the great creation hymns of the Psalter, this psalm is a companion to Psalm 103's hymn of redemption. It stands within Book IV, Psalms 90 to 106, which answers Book III's crisis over the apparent failure of the Davidic covenant by returning to the eternal kingship and creative power of the LORD Himself. Structured as a meditation that follows the days of Genesis 1, from light and sky to land, creatures, and human labor, it turns the creation account itself into personal praise.
section.thesis: The LORD who wraps Himself in light, founds the earth, and waters every valley sustains all His creatures moment by moment from His open hand, and by His Spirit continually renews the face of the ground, so that the fitting end of such a world is not human self-sufficiency but a lifetime of praise.
section.frameNote: Six movements: praise to the majestic Creator, verses 1 to 4; the earth's foundations and the flood's appointed boundary, verses 5 to 9; provision for every living creature, verses 10 to 18; sun, moon, and the rhythm of labor, verses 19 to 23; the wide sea and the sustaining Spirit, verses 24 to 30; and a closing doxology with a vow of lifelong praise, verses 31 to 35.

units.label: Bless the LORD, O my soul

verses.text: Bless the LORD, O my soul! O LORD my God, You are very great; You are clothed with splendor and {{hadar|majesty}}.
He wraps Himself in light as with a garment; He stretches out the heavens like a tent,
laying the beams of His chambers in the waters above, making the clouds His chariot, walking on the wings of the wind.
He makes the winds His messengers, flames of fire His servants.

units.label: He set the earth on its foundations
units.note: the earth's stability and the flood's appointed boundary

verses.text: He set the earth on its foundations, never to be moved.
You covered it with the deep like a garment; the waters stood above the mountains.
At Your rebuke the waters fled; at the sound of Your thunder they hurried away,
the mountains rose and the valleys sank to the place You assigned for them,
You set a boundary they cannot cross, that they may never again cover the earth.

units.label: He waters the mountains from His chambers

verses.text: He sends forth springs in the valleys; they flow between the mountains.
They give drink to every beast of the field; the wild donkeys quench their thirst.
The birds of the air nest beside the springs; they sing among the branches.
He waters the mountains from His chambers; the earth is satisfied by the fruit of His works.
He makes the grass grow for the livestock and provides crops for man to cultivate, bringing forth food from the earth:
wine that gladdens the heart of man, oil that makes his face to shine, and bread that sustains his heart.
The trees of the LORD have their fill, the cedars of Lebanon that He planted,
where the birds build their nests; the stork makes her home in the cypresses.
The high mountains are for the wild goats, the cliffs a refuge for the rock badgers.

units.label: He made the moon to mark the seasons
units.note: sun, moon, and the rhythm of labor

verses.text: He made the moon to mark the seasons; the sun knows when to set.
You bring darkness, and it becomes night, when all the beasts of the forest prowl.
The young lions roar for their prey and seek their food from God.
The sun rises, and they withdraw; they lie down in their dens.
Man goes forth to his work and to his labor until evening.

units.label: How many are Your works, O LORD
units.note: the wide sea and the sustaining Spirit

verses.text: How many are Your works, O LORD! In wisdom You have made them all; the earth is full of Your creatures.
Here is the sea, vast and wide, teeming with creatures beyond number, living things both great and small.
There the ships pass, and {{leviathan|Leviathan}}, which You formed to frolic there.
All creatures look to You to give them their food in due season.
When You give it to them, they gather it up; when You open Your hand, they are satisfied with good things.
When You hide Your face, they are terrified; when You take away their breath, they die and return to dust.
When You send Your {{ruach|Spirit}}, they are created, and You renew the face of the earth.

units.label: Bless the LORD, O my soul
units.note: doxology and a vow of lifelong praise

verses.text: May the glory of the LORD endure forever; may the LORD rejoice in His works.
He looks on the earth, and it trembles; He touches the mountains, and they smolder.
I will sing to the LORD all my life; I will sing praise to my God while I have my being.
May my meditation be pleasing to Him, for I rejoice in the LORD.
May sinners vanish from the earth and the wicked be no more. Bless the LORD, O my soul. Hallelujah!

hadar.gloss: Splendor, majesty, honor. Paired with hod, splendor, in verse 1, it pictures the LORD arrayed as a king in the very radiance and order of creation itself.

leviathan.gloss: A great sea creature, feared in the myths of Israel's neighbors as a chaos monster. The psalm strips away the terror, Leviathan is simply a creature the LORD formed to frolic in the sea He rules, compare Job 41.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The same Spirit who hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2 continually creates and renews the face of the ground; life itself is sustained breath by breath from God alone.

basic.context: This psalm is a hymn of praise that walks through creation the way Genesis 1 does, light and sky, land and water, plants and creatures, sun and moon, and finally man at his labor. It is not abstract theology but a personal act of worship, the psalmist stirring his own soul to bless the LORD for a world he still finds astonishing.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes God's ongoing sustaining care, not merely His past act of creation. Every creature depends on Him moment by moment, verse 27 to 30, and the whole psalm calls the reader to the same response the psalmist models, wonder that turns into praise rather than into pride at human mastery over nature.
basic.originalLanguages: God is clothed with hadar, majesty, verse 1, the very dress of a king. The Spirit, ruach, verse 30, is the same breath that hovered over creation in Genesis 1:2 and that continually renews the ground. Even Leviathan, livyatan, verse 26, feared elsewhere as chaos itself, is simply God's plaything in a sea He fully rules.
basic.typology: The winds and flames who serve as God's messengers, verse 4, are cited in Hebrews 1:7 as part of the case for the Son's supremacy over the angels. The renewing Spirit, verse 30, anticipates the Spirit's work of new birth and the future renewal of all creation.
basic.christ: Colossians 1:16-17 says all things were created through Christ and for Him, and in Him all things hold together, the fullest statement of what this psalm pictures in God's sustaining hand. Hebrews 1:7 quotes verse 4 directly to show that the Son who made the angels His servants is worshipped, not merely served by, the created order.
basic.god: God is shown as majestic Creator and patient Sustainer together, He who founded the earth and set the sea's boundary, and who still opens His hand every day so that every creature, without exception, is fed. His pleasure in His own works, verse 31, shows a God who delights in what He has made.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1 supplies the days of creation that this psalm turns into praise. Hebrews 1:7 quotes verse 4 concerning the Son's supremacy over angels. Colossians 1:16-17 states what this psalm pictures, that all things hold together in Christ. Romans 8:19-22 speaks of creation's longing for the very renewal this psalm attributes to God's Spirit.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's hymn to the Creator who sustains every creature and renews the face of the ground by His Spirit finds its fulfillment in Christ, through whom and for whom all things were made and in whom all things hold together, and in the Spirit who will one day renew not just the ground but the whole creation.

context.literary: A creation hymn structured in six movements that trace the days of Genesis 1, opening and closing with the same call, Bless the LORD, O my soul, an inclusio that frames the entire psalm as personal worship rather than detached description.
context.historical: Likely sung in temple worship, perhaps at harvest festivals when Israel celebrated God's goodness in creation, compare Deuteronomy 16:13-15. No author is named in the Hebrew text, though Jewish and early Christian tradition often associated it with David alongside Psalm 103.
context.canonical: Paired naturally with Psalm 103, together framing creation and redemption, and with Psalm 105, which follows the same 'Bless the LORD' pattern but turns from creation to covenant history. Its language undergirds later biblical praise for God's ongoing providence, Matthew 6:26, Acts 14:15, Acts 17:24-28.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn contemplation of the created order, in all its scope from the heavens to the smallest creature, into a personal act of praise, and to ground human confidence not in self-sufficiency but in daily dependence on God's sustaining hand.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the created order and God's ongoing care for it; it prescribes as an abiding pattern the discipline of noticing God's provision in ordinary things, food, seasons, work, and rest, and letting that notice become worship.

debates.question: Is Leviathan in verse 26 a literal sea creature or primarily a symbolic figure of chaos?

views.label: A literal great sea creature
views.case: The verse sits among concrete descriptions of ships and sea life; Job 38 to 41 catalogs real, if formidable, creatures, and Leviathan fits naturally among them as an actual animal, likely a whale or crocodile-like creature.

views.label: A symbolic figure drawn from ancient chaos imagery
views.case: In the wider ancient Near East, sea monsters like Leviathan represented untamable chaos; the psalm may be deliberately using that loaded image to make a theological point about God's total mastery over what pagan neighbors feared as uncontrollable.

debates.bearing: Either way the psalm's point stands undiminished: whatever was feared as chaotic and untamable, God simply formed for His pleasure and Leviathan's play, showing there is no power, real or imagined, outside His sovereign rule.

study.originalLanguages: God is clothed with hadar, majesty, verse 1, and He sends His ruach, Spirit or breath, verse 30, the same Spirit active in Genesis 1:2, to create and renew the ground continually. Even livyatan, Leviathan, feared elsewhere as chaos itself, is simply formed by God to frolic in a sea entirely under His rule.

typology.type: The winds as God's messengers, flames of fire His servants, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: Cited in Hebrews 1:7 as part of the argument that the Son, unlike the angels who merely serve, is worshipped and reigns over all creation.

typology.type: The Spirit who renews the face of the ground, verse 30
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the Spirit's work of new birth, Titus 3:5, and the future renewal of all creation, Romans 8:19-22, 2 Corinthians 5:17.

study.christ: Colossians 1:16-17 states that all things were created through Christ and for Him, and in Him all things hold together, the New Testament's fullest statement of what this psalm pictures in God's sustaining hand over every creature. Hebrews 1:7 quotes verse 4 directly, showing that the Son who commands the winds and flames as His servants is Himself worshipped as God, not merely one more created thing declaring His glory.
study.god: God is majestic Creator and patient Sustainer in the same breath, He who founded the earth so it will never be moved, yet who still opens His hand every day so that every creature, without exception, is fed in its season. His own pleasure in His works, verse 31, reveals a God who delights in what He has made, and whose renewing Spirit, verse 30, keeps giving life rather than abandoning it to decay.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Psalm 104:4, He makes His angels winds and His ministers a flame of fire, part of the case for the Son's supremacy over the angels.
All things were created through Christ and for Him, and in Him all things hold together, the fullest statement of what this psalm pictures in the sustaining hand of God.
Supplies the days of creation, light, sky, land, creatures, and humanity, that this psalm turns into a hymn of personal praise.
Creation's longing for renewal from decay echoes this psalm's picture of God renewing the face of the ground by His Spirit.

study.oneStory: This psalm's hymn to the Creator who sustains every creature and renews the face of the ground by His Spirit finds its fulfillment in Christ, through whom and for whom all things were made and in whom all things hold together, and in the Spirit who will one day renew not just the ground but the whole creation.

sermon.title: The Pleasure of God in His Creation
sermon.why: Piper works directly through Psalm 104, especially verse 31, to show that God genuinely delights in what He has made, and that this pleasure is not idolatry but the overflow of the Father and Son's joy in each other, pressing the psalm's own movement from creation to worship of the Creator Himself.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say happens when God opens His hand toward His creatures, verses 27-28?

options.text: They gather what He gives and are satisfied with good things.
options.note: Every creature depends moment by moment on God's provision, not on its own securing of resources.

options.text: They immediately die and return to dust.
options.note: That describes the opposite scene, when God hides His face and takes away breath, verse 29.

options.text: They begin to build their own shelters.
options.note: The text is about receiving food from God's hand, not self-sufficient building.

options.text: They multiply beyond what the land can support.
options.note: Close to the theme of abundance, but the verse specifically names satisfaction with good things, not overpopulation.

quiz.q: According to verse 30, what renews the face of the ground?

options.text: God sending forth His Spirit.
options.note: The same Spirit active in Genesis 1:2 continually creates and renews; life and renewal come from God alone.

options.text: The changing of the seasons by human effort.
options.note: The verse credits God's Spirit, not human agricultural effort, with renewal.

options.text: The return of the rains after a drought.
options.note: Rain appears elsewhere in the psalm, but this verse specifically names God's Spirit as the source of renewal.

options.text: The migration of the animals to new habitats.
options.note: Animal habitats are described earlier in the psalm, but verse 30 is specifically about the Spirit renewing the ground.

quiz.q: What is Leviathan doing in the sea, according to verse 26?

options.text: Frolicking, formed by God to play there.
options.note: The psalm strips away the fear surrounding this creature, showing it as simply God's plaything in a sea fully under His rule.

options.text: Threatening to overwhelm the ships and destroy them.
options.note: The verse presents Leviathan as playful, not as a threat to shipping.

options.text: Hiding from the sun in the depths.
options.note: The text says Leviathan frolics, formed for that purpose by God, not that it is hiding.

options.text: Guarding the boundary between sea and land.
options.note: That boundary-setting role belongs to God Himself, verses 8-9, not to Leviathan.

quiz.q: What is the psalmist's resolve in verse 33?

options.text: To sing to the LORD all his life and sing praise to his God while he has his being.
options.note: The psalm moves from observing creation to a personal, lifelong vow of praise.

options.text: To catalog every species of creature he has observed.
options.note: The psalm has described many creatures, but verse 33 is a vow of praise, not a scientific resolve.

options.text: To build a temple in honor of creation.
options.note: No such building project is mentioned; the resolve is to sing and praise, not construct.

options.text: To retire from his work and rest permanently.
options.note: The psalm honors both labor and rest, verses 22-23, but verse 33's specific resolve is lifelong singing and praise.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament draw directly on this psalm to describe Christ?

options.text: Hebrews 1:7 quotes Psalm 104:4 to show the Son's supremacy over the angels who serve as winds and flames.
options.note: Hebrews 1 uses this very verse as part of its argument that the Son is worshipped, not merely one more servant like the angels.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Hebrews 1:7 directly quotes this psalm, and Colossians 1:16-17 states the same truth of Christ sustaining all things.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of the new creation.
options.note: The psalm speaks of God's renewing Spirit and coming judgment on the wicked, not a specific date.

options.text: It is fulfilled only by modern advances in agriculture.
options.note: The psalm's deepest fulfillment is in Christ sustaining creation, Colossians 1:17, not in human technological progress alone.

questions: The psalm moves from the vastness of the heavens to God's minute care for badgers and birds. Where do you need to remember God's personal, detailed provision for you this week?
Verses 27 to 29 picture every creature waiting on God's open hand for its food and breath. What are you trying to secure for yourself that you actually need to receive from His hand instead?

The psalmist prays that his meditation would be pleasing to God and that he would rejoice in the LORD Himself, not merely in creation, verse 34. Are you enjoying God's gifts more than God Himself right now?
Verse 30 shows the Spirit renewing the face of the ground. Where in your own life, whether in weariness, sin, or grief, do you need that same renewing work of God's Spirit?

The psalm ends longing for sinners to vanish and the wicked to be no more, even in the middle of such wonder at creation. How does knowing creation itself groans for renewal, Romans 8:19-22, shape your hope for the brokenness you see in the world?

Piper's sermon notes that God delights in works no human eye will ever see, like creatures at the bottom of the sea. How does it change your prayer life to know that your private, unseen faithfulness is also seen and enjoyed by God?

translationNotes.note: The Septuagint renders this, He makes His angels winds, His servants flames of fire, the wording quoted in Hebrews 1:7; both readings show creation's forces obeying God's command as His servants.
Cypresses may also be rendered pines, junipers, or firs; the precise tree is uncertain in Hebrew, but the picture of God providing a safe habitat for the stork stands regardless.
Spirit can also be rendered breath; either way, the text credits God alone, not natural cycles, with creating and renewing life on the ground.
